danger_signals.meaning: Neighboring controlled territories represent military threat vectors
Visible troop concentrations in provinces indicate imminent invasion danger
Loss of alliance status and broken treaties signal increased conflict probability

safety_signals.meaning: Neighboring allied provinces provide defensive buffer against attack
Healthy province resources indicate capacity to defend territory
Active alliances and non-aggression pacts reduce threat from specific factions

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Province ownership display indicates faction control of territories across China map
Distinct provincial territories assigned to different player-controlled or AI factions

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: All provinces maintain assigned colors/ownership markers without change
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Factions engage in diplomacy, economic development, troop training

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Province borders shift as territories change faction control during battles
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Factions move armies between provinces, conduct sieges, lose/gain territory

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Relationship status indicators change between faction pairs
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Factions initiate covert actions, bribery, espionage, and alliance negotiations

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Province prosperity or resource availability indicators reflect financial strain
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Factions reduce military spending, engage in trade negotiations, seek economic alliances

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: One faction's color/marker controls majority of visible provinces on map
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Dominant faction expands aggressively; other factions form counter-alliances

environmental_storytelling.example: The China map shows territorial ownership through visual differentiation, immediately communicating political control and fragmentation state
References to Three Kingdoms period evoke historical context for players, establishing that political struggle is the core narrative
Province resources and population numbers indicate which territories are economically valuable vs. strategically important
Visible troop counts in provinces communicate military capability and readiness for conflict

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Seeks alliances with other factions, initiates defensive troop movements, engages in diplomatic overtures
Expands military, launches offensive campaigns, demands tribute, breaks treaties with weaker neighbors
Engages in bribery negotiations, offers trade agreements, attempts espionage to reduce enemy strength
Can initiate sieges, demand surrender, launch invasions with acceptable casualty ratios
Province control shifts after resolved combat; visual ownership changes to victorious faction

notes: Romance of the Three Kingdoms (1988 NES version) is a turn-based strategy game where visual communication of world-state occurs primarily through the provincial map display system. The game communicates political situation through: (1) territory ownership coloring/markers showing faction dominance and fragmentation, (2) numerical displays of troop strength, resources, and populations indicating power asymmetries, (3) diplomatic status indicators showing alliance vs. enemy relationships, and (4) economic metrics reflecting prosperity or crisis. Danger is communicated implicitly through proximity of hostile territories and explicit through military force comparisons. The game emphasizes non-military victory conditions (economics, intrigue, diplomacy, covert action) as alternatives to conquest, suggesting a complex political communication layer beyond simple territorial control visualization. NES limitations mean visual manifestations are abstract/symbolic rather than representational.
